_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 1
questions.prompt: What is the main job of a constructor?
choices: Establish a valid starting state
Delete the object
Make every member public
Run after every method
questions.explanation: The constructor initializes the new object so its public operations can rely on a valid state.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 2
choices: It promises not to modify the object through that method
It makes the returned integer constant forever
It hides the method
It allocates the object
questions.explanation: The trailing const lets callers and the compiler know that reading the balance does not change the account.

questions.title: Trace Classes & Objects
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The constructor initializes the balance, deposit changes it through a rule, and the const getter reads it without modifying the object. The resulting output is '25'.

questions.title: Classes & Objects good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Classes & Objects?
choices: Give the class one clear responsibility.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Give the class one clear responsibility. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: Public data lets any caller create an invalid state. A small public interface can enforce the class rule in one place.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Initialize every member'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.

A constructor body runs after data members have already been initialized. Prefer the member initializer list for their starting values, especially for const members and objects that have no default constructor.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: A constructor body runs after data members have already been initialized. Prefer the member initializer list for their starting values, especially for const members and objects that have no default constructor.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Classes & Objects?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Initialize every member in the constructor.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Initialize every member in the constructor. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Classes & Objects?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Keep invariants behind private data.
questions.explanation: Keep invariants behind private data. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Classes & Objects?
choices: Mark read-only methods const.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Mark read-only methods const. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 10
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The constructor initializes the balance, deposit changes it through a rule, and the const getter reads it without modifying the object. The output is '25'.
